title: Two `/sys/fs/cgroup` mounts in a rootless container in a rootless container
body:
### Issue Description

I try to fully grasp all the possible combinations of cgroups behaviour that can happen with podman, so I run possibly strange combination of tests.

When I have a privileged rootless podman container and I run a rootless podman container it in, in a nested user namespace, privileged or unprivileged, I see two `/sys/fs/cgroup` mounts.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. With podman-4.8.3-1.fc39.x86_64, run a privileged rootless `quay.io/podman/stable` container:
```
host$ podman run --rm -ti --privileged -h container quay.io/podman/stable
[root@container /]#
```
2. Check that we run in user namespace and what the cgroups situation is:
```
[root@container /]# cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0       1000          1
         1     100000      65536
[root@container /]# mount | grep cgroup
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
[root@container /]# mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/test-123 && ls -dl /sys/fs/cgroup/test-123
drwxr-xr-x. 2 root root 0 Jan 26 08:32 /sys/fs/cgroup/test-123
```
3. As the `podman` user in that container, run a container in a container:
```
[root@container /]# runuser -u podman -- podman run --rm -ti --uts=private -h nested registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora
Trying to pull registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:latest...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 718a00fe3212 done   | 
Copying config 368a084ba1 done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination
WARN[0010] Path "/run/secrets/etc-pki-entitlement" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
WARN[0010] Path "/run/secrets/rhsm" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
[root@nested /]
```
4. Check that we run in another (nested) user namespace:
```
[root@nested /]# cat /proc/self/uid_map 
         0       1000          1
         1          1        999
      1000       1001      64535
```
5. Check what cgroups mounts we have
```
[root@nested /]# mount | grep cgroup
```

### Describe the results you received

```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```

Additional check
```
[root@nested /]# mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/test-123456
mkdir: cannot create directory ‘/sys/fs/cgroup/test-123456’: Read-only file system
```
shows that the read-only mount "wins".

(Note that this issues is not about the mount being read-only, per se, it's about there being two of them.)

### Describe the results you expected

```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```
or
```
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```
but not both.

### podman info output

```yaml
[root@container /]# podman info

  arch: amd64

  cgroupManager: cgroupfs
```

### Podman in a container

Yes

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

This is deterministic.

comments.body:
It seems this has something to do with the default `container.conf` in that `quay.io/podman/stable` image. When I remove that file, the behaviour changes to the expected one:
```
[root@container /]# cat /etc/containers/containers.conf 
[containers]
netns="host"
userns="host"
ipcns="host"
utsns="host"
cgroupns="host"
cgroups="disabled"
log_driver = "k8s-file"
[engine]
cgroup_manager = "cgroupfs"
events_logger="file"
runtime="crun"
[root@container /]# runuser -u podman -- podman run --rm --uts=private -h nested registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora mount | grep cgroup
WARN[0000] Path "/run/secrets/etc-pki-entitlement" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
WARN[0000] Path "/run/secrets/rhsm" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
[root@container /]# rm /etc/containers/containers.conf
rm: remove regular file '/etc/containers/containers.conf'? y
[root@container /]# runuser -u podman -- podman run --rm --uts=private -h nested registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora mount | grep cgroup
WARN[0000] Path "/run/secrets/etc-pki-entitlement" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
WARN[0000] Path "/run/secrets/rhsm" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
cgroup2 on /sys/fs/cgroup type cgroup2 (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot)
```

if you do not create a new network namespace (the `netns="host"` in the default configuration file), it is not possible to mount a fresh sysfs inside the user namespace, so it is bind mounted from the current environment (in this case, the outer container).

If you need a fresh sys mount, then you need to pass `--net=private`, or `--net=none`.
What is the purpose of that bound mount? Wouldn't the original content (that the process gets from its parent) work just fine?

when we bind mount `/sys` from the outer container, we must use `MS_REC` and mount it recursively.  This brings in  the `/sys/fs/cgroup` from the outer environment.

We always mount a fresh `/sys/fs/cgroup` in a container, that is hard-coded in Podman, the new mount ends up on top of the one we bind mounted from `/sys`.

It is probably clearer if you look at the output of `findmnt -R /sys`:

```
# runuser -u podman -- podman run --rm -ti --uts=private -h nested fedora sh -c 'findmnt -R /sys'
WARN[0000] Path "/run/secrets/etc-pki-entitlement" from "/etc/containers/mounts.conf" doesn't exist, skipping 
TARGET                          SOURCE  FSTYPE  OPTIONS
/sys                            sysfs   sysfs   ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel
|-/sys/fs/cgroup                cgroup2 cgroup2 rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot
| `-/sys/fs/cgroup              cgroup2 cgroup2 ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot
|-/sys/kernel                   tmpfs   tmpfs   ro,relatime,seclabel,size=0k,uid=100999,gid=100999,inode64
|-/sys/firmware                 tmpfs   tmpfs   ro,relatime,seclabel,size=0k,uid=100999,gid=100999,inode64
|-/sys/fs/selinux               tmpfs   tmpfs   ro,relatime,seclabel,size=0k,uid=100999,gid=100999,inode64
|-/sys/dev/block                tmpfs   tmpfs   ro,relatime,seclabel,size=0k,uid=100999,gid=100999,inode64
`-/sys/devices/virtual/powercap tmpfs   tmpfs   ro,relatime,seclabel,size=0k,uid=100999,gid=100999,inode64

```
Ah, so it stems from the `/sys` handling, and `/sys/fs/cgroup` then "patches" on top of what `MS_REC` brought.
Would unmounting the original `/sys/fs/cgroup` bind-mount before doing the fresh one be unreasonable?

you can't unmount the `/sys/fs/cgroup` mount from the outer container, as it is part of the `/sys` mount.  You can drop only the second one that was mounted on top of it:

```
# runuser -u podman -- podman run --privileged --rm -ti --uts=private -h nested registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora sh -c 'umount /sys/fs/cgroup; umount /sys/fs/cgroup; findmnt -R /sys'
TARGET           SOURCE  FSTYPE  OPTIONS
/sys             sysfs   sysfs   rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel
`-/sys/fs/cgroup cgroup2 cgroup2 rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime,seclabel,nsdelegate,memory_recursiveprot
```
